title: Spot your triggers
description: The button that gets pushed — and how to see it coming.
summaryPoints: Name a trigger: a word, person, or moment that sets off a bigger reaction than it deserves
Spot the pattern — what happened right before you flared, and what it made you feel about yourself
Catch the body's spike (heat, racing heart) as your early warning
Triggers aren't a flaw — everyone has them, and naming yours takes their power
The reaction is automatic; the response is yours to choose once you see it coming

content.title: The button
content.body:
Someone says one little thing — "calm down," or "whatever" — and suddenly you're way more heated than the moment calls for. That's a **trigger**: a word, look, person, or memory that sets off a reaction bigger than the situation deserves.

It feels huge because it's not really about right now. It's poking an old, sore spot.
content.imageAlt: A lit match touching a fuse, like an emotional trigger catching

content.title: Why it lands so hard
content.body:
A trigger taps an old sensitivity — usually a feeling like:
- **disrespected** ("you don't matter")
- **excluded** ("you're not wanted")
- **controlled** ("you can't choose")
- **judged** or **not good enough**

So when a friend cuts you off mid-sentence, the spark isn't really the interruption. It's the old story it whispers underneath.

content.prompt: What actually makes something a trigger?
content.context: Your sibling rolls their eyes at your idea and you're instantly furious.
options: It sets off a reaction bigger than the moment, tapping an old sore spot
It only happens to people who are too sensitive
It's a sign the other person is always the problem
It means you don't actually have any feelings about it
content.explanation: A trigger hits hard because it pokes an old sensitivity — the size of the reaction is the clue something deeper got touched.

content.prompt: Only weak or overly sensitive people have triggers.
content.explanation: Everyone has triggers — they're part of being human. The skill isn't getting rid of them, it's spotting yours.

content.prompt: Match each trigger to the deeper need it's poking.

content.title: Find your pattern
content.body:
Triggers love to repeat. To catch yours, rewind the tape after a flare-up and ask two things:

- **What happened right before?** The actual moment you reacted to.
- **What did it make me feel about myself?** Small? Ignored? Bossed around?

Do that a few times and you'll see the same sore spot show up again and again.

content.prompt: Common trigger theme, or just a neutral event?

items.text: Being ignored or left on read

content.prompt: A trigger sets off a reaction that's ___ than the moment actually deserves.
content.hint: Think about the size of the reaction.
content.explanation: The oversized reaction is the tell — it's the old sore spot reacting, not just the moment.

content.title: Your early warning system
content.body:
Before your brain even has words, your **body** sounds the alarm. The spike shows up as:
- a rush of heat in your face or chest
- a racing or pounding heart
- a tight jaw, clenched fists, or held breath

That physical jolt is your early warning. Catch it, and you've got a half-second head start before the reaction takes the wheel.

content.prompt: What's the earliest warning that you've been triggered?
options: A body spike — heat, a racing heart, a tight jaw
content.explanation: Your body reacts before your thoughts do. That physical spike is the first sign — and your chance to catch the trigger early.

content.instructions: One word makes this false. Tap it.
content.sentence: Your first gut reaction to a trigger is chosen in the heat of the moment.
content.explanation: The first reaction is automatic — it fires before you decide. The response that comes after is the part you actually get to choose.

content.title: Name it to tame it
content.body:
Once you can say it out loud — "I get defensive when I feel blamed" — the trigger loses its grip.

Naming it turns an ambush into something you saw coming. Now you can plan: take a breath, ask a question, step away for a minute. The reaction is automatic. The response is yours — **if** you see the trigger first.

content.prompt: Put the trigger-handling steps in order.
steps: Notice the body spike — heat, racing heart
Name the trigger — "I feel blamed right now"
Choose your response instead of reacting
content.explanation: Catch the body's signal, name what got poked, then you've got room to choose — not just react.

content.prompt: Name one real trigger of yours, the early body sign you'd feel, and one thing you'll do when you spot it.
content.context: No wrong answers here — this is just for you. The more honest, the more useful.
content.placeholder: When I feel ___, my body ___, so I'll ___
content.explanation: You just turned an automatic ambush into something you can see coming. That's the whole skill — naming it is taming it.
